project.title: PTBP1 and PTBP2 associate with distinct proteins and have distinct post-translational modifications in nuclear neuronal extract.
project.projectDescription: PTBP1 and PTBP2 are highly conserved paralogs, yet exhibit distinct splicing patterns. Here we use mass spectrometry to show that PTBP1 and PTBP2 interact with distinct proteins and undergo distinct post translational modifications under splicing conditions in neuronal nuclear extract.
project.sampleProcessingProtocol: Purified histidine-tagged protein was incubated in in vitro splicing reaction mixture containing neuronal nuclear extract. For protein-protein interactions, Nickel binding beads were added and non-specifically bound protein was washed away. Samples underwent dialysis following guidelines provided by the UCSD Mass spectrometry facility. Samples then underwent standard preparation for LUMOS Orbitrap mass spectrometry. For PTM analysis, Protein was run on a NuPAGE 4-12% Bis-Tris gel and extracted and sent to the UCSD Mass Sprectrometry Facility where it underwent standard preparation for TimTOF pro 2 mass specrometry.
project.dataProcessingProtocol: Only peptides with areas greater than "0" were used. Proteins and PTMs found in the control buffers were removed, along with known common contaminants. Gene ontology was used to determine the biological and molecular function of the proteins. For PTM, the rate of modification was determined by dividing the number of modified amino acids by the amount of times that particular amino acid appears.

references.referenceLine: Sullivan ME, Edberg JA, Nunez CI, Axelrod HL, Keppetipola NM. Polypyrimidine tract binding proteins PTBP1 and PTBP2 associate with distinct proteins and have distinct post-translational modifications in neuronal nuclear extract. PLoS One. 2025 20(6):e0325143

Characteristics[Specimen]: Buffer DG (20 mM HEPES KOH pH 7.9, 20% glycerol, 80 mM KCl, 0.2 mM PMSF, 1 mM DTT)
Comment[Instrument]: Orbitrap fusion Lumos hybrid mass spectrometer (Thermo)
Comment[SpecilizedMS]: starting from the PASEF method for standard proteomics
Comment[CollisionEnergy]: The values for mobility-dependent collision energy ramping were set to 95 eV at an inversed reduced mobility (1/k0) of 1.6 V s/cm2 and 23 eV at 0.73 V s/cm2
